scored_pairs.sentence: B (team separation strategy) is a solution instance of the diagnostic framework in A (identify true constraint upstream)
Both address positioning vs team structure as scaling constraints, but no direct causal link
A addresses revenue structure; B addresses team structure—both enable scaling but on different axes
Both discuss filtering/segmentation (teams vs pricing) as mechanisms to maximize segment-specific economics
B describes a specific failure case of A's principle: misallocating founder energy across channels
Both describe founder resource allocation patterns; B is a specific channel case of A's generic bottleneck
Both illustrate tradeoffs in business model design and resource constraint
B (lazy sales reps from over-supply) is a specific mechanism explaining A's funnel friction diagnosis
B exemplifies A's principle: over-supplied leads reduce cold-call rigor and process discipline
A addresses insufficient supply (hire/restructure to scale); B addresses over-supply (efficiency collapse)
A discusses founder selling model; B discusses compensation structure—both relevant to sales scaling but independent
B (equity as mistake) reinforces A's thesis: founder must become trainer/leader, not co-owner with sales staff
Both enable sales scaling but at different levels: process optimization vs compensation structure
B (ROI of hiring salespeople) is the economic justification for A's compensation philosophy
Both frame hiring salespeople as capital allocation with measurable ROI; B is the foundational principle
A discusses commission liquidity; B discusses compensation structure—both relevant but no direct link
B (equity mistake) reinforces A's principle: founder salesperson value cannot be replicated via ownership stakes
Both assert value is context-dependent; B is personal/financial variant of A's general principle
B (save/spend ratio as risk proxy) operationalizes A's insight (earnings correlate with risk tolerance)
B (savings as risk proxy) supports A's claim that risk-taking becomes the scarce human trait
B operationalizes A's principle: showing how wealth affects spending via compounding math
Both use $500 price point but in different contexts (CAC math vs opportunity cost); surface coincidence
Both reference $500 pricing but apply it to different principles (price bands vs opportunity cost)
A discusses pricing strategy (anchoring); B discusses personal spending opportunity cost—distinct domains
B describes wealth tiers where A's opportunity-cost logic applies differently at scale
A addresses individual wealth leverage; B addresses market segmentation by wealth—related but separate
A is tactical (facility move timing); B is strategic (personal wealth psychology)—parallel logic, different domain
B (opportunity cost of spending) is the foundational principle enabling A's time-value calculations
Both use $500 price point but in different contexts (CAC math vs opportunity cost); surface coincidence

Both identify low-return activities but A is business economics, B is personal finance—parallel structure
Both address capital recycling logic but at different scales (business model vs personal savings)
B (unit economics at scale) requires A's personal capital discipline to fund acquisition
Both apply compounding/opportunity-cost logic; B is the mathematical principle enabling A's season-scarcity thesis
B's opportunity-cost framework explains why A's 30-day CAC payback is critical

A (premium tier reinvestment) exemplifies B's principle (commit to fixed investment before lifestyle)

Both assert same principle: commit to savings/investment before allocating to expenses
A is acquisition math; B is wealth psychology—related but on different axes
B's commitment psychology principle explains A's mechanism: sunk cost changes behavior
A describes business trap; B describes wealth strategy—parallel thinking but different domains
